text:
PSMA PET/CT (Gallium-68 PSMA-11)

EXAMINATION: Gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT, whole body with diagnostic CT

INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (Grade Group 5), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant. Prior treatments include ADT + Zytiga/prednisone (progressed), Xtandi (progressed with new osseous metastases in thoracic spine and rib), and palliative radiation to T10-T12. Currently approximately 4 months into ola 200 mg twice daily (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID due to grade 3 anemia requiring transfusion support). Restaging to assess treatment response and guide ongoing management.

COMPARISON: Technetium-99m bone scan performed approximately 9 months prior (at time of Xtandi progression); CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 4 months prior (prior to ola initiation, showing pelvic nodal regression then re-growth).

TECHNIQUE: 148 MBq Ga-68 PSMA-11 administered intravenously. PET imaging acquired 60 minutes post-injection from vertex to mid-thigh, fused with low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Diagnostic-quality CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired with intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL, 120 mL).

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No PSMA-avid lesions in the calvarium, skull base, or cervical lymph nodes. Minor physiologic salivary gland and lacrimal gland PSMA uptake within normal limits. No pathologic cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy by CT.

CHEST:
Lungs: Bilateral pulmonary parenchyma clear on diagnostic CT. No pulmonary nodules identified. No pleural effusions. No endobronchial lesions. No PSMA-avid pulmonary lesions.
Mediastinum and Hila: Heart size normal. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pericardial effusion.
Chest wall and ribs: Right posterior 4th rib lesion demonstrates post-radiation/sclerotic changes with very faint, low-level residual PSMA uptake (SUVmax approximately 1.8), substantially diminished compared to expected activity based on prior imaging. T10 and T12 vertebral bodies show post-treatment changes with reduced trabeculae density on CT and low-level PSMA activity (SUVmax 1.6 and 1.4, respectively), consistent with treatment effect following palliative radiation and ongoing systemic therapy. No new rib or sternal lesions.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No focal hepatic lesions identified on either diagnostic CT or PSMA PET. The liver is homogeneous in density and shows only background physiologic PSMA uptake. No intrahepatic biliary dilation.
Spleen: Normal in size, no PSMA-avid lesions.
Pancreas: Unremarkable. No pancreatic lesions.
Kidneys: Bilateral kidneys with expected physiologic PSMA excretion into collecting systems. No obstructive uropathy. No renal cortical lesions. Stable bilateral simple renal cysts (largest right kidney 1.4 cm).
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands without PSMA-avid lesions. No CT-defined adrenal masses.
Gastrointestinal: Colon and small bowel unremarkable without focal abnormality or bowel wall thickening. Physiologic PSMA activity in hepatic flexure and sigmoid.

RETROPERITONEUM AND PELVIS:
Lymph nodes: Previously documented right obturator node (1.9 cm on prior CT) now measures approximately 1.1 cm with only mild PSMA uptake (SUVmax 2.4, near background). Left external iliac node similarly decreased in size, now 0.7 cm with no convincing PSMA avidity above background. No new pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Para-aortic chain: no PSMA-avid nodes.
Prostate bed: No focal PSMA-avid lesion in the prostate bed. Prostate gland heterogeneous with low-level diffuse activity, consistent with prior treatment effect.
Bladder: Physiologic tracer excretion within bladder lumen. No bladder wall lesion.

SKELETON:
Lumbar spine (L2, L3, L4): Known osseous metastases demonstrate sclerotic CT morphology consistent with treatment effect. PSMA uptake substantially reduced compared to expected appearance of active disease: L2 SUVmax 3.1, L3 SUVmax 2.8, L4 SUVmax 2.6. These values reflect a significant decrease in metabolic activity relative to prior bone scan findings of progressive lesions.
Pelvis: Previously documented lesions in the bilateral sacroiliac joints and right iliac wing show sclerotic remodeling on CT. PSMA avidity markedly reduced: right sacroiliac joint SUVmax 2.2, right iliac wing SUVmax 1.9. Left posterior iliac lesion similarly low-level (SUVmax 1.7). No new pelvic osseous lesions.
Right femur: Lesion at femoral neck and proximal shaft demonstrates sclerotic CT changes with low-level PSMA activity (SUVmax 2.1). Cortical integrity maintained. No pathologic fracture identified.
Thoracic spine (other levels): T10 and T12 as above (post-radiation, low uptake). Remainder of thoracic spine without new PSMA-avid foci. No new thoracic vertebral lesions.
Cervical spine: No PSMA-avid osseous lesions in cervical vertebrae or posterior elements.
Shoulder girdle, upper extremities: No PSMA-avid lesions identified.
Lower extremities (below mid-thigh): Not imaged in full per protocol.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked reduction in PSMA avidity throughout all previously documented osseous metastatic sites including lumbar spine, pelvis, sacroiliac joints, and right femur, consistent with a substantial metabolic treatment response to Lynparza. CT morphology showing sclerotic changes supports this interpretation.

2. Post-radiation changes at T10, T12 vertebral bodies and right posterior 4th rib with minimal residual PSMA activity, consistent with combined effects of prior palliative radiotherapy and ongoing systemic therapy.

3. Notable reduction in size and PSMA avidity of the right obturator pelvic lymph node compared to prior CT (1.9 cm previously, now 1.1 cm, with near-background uptake), suggesting favorable nodal response.

4. No hepatic lesions identified on either PET or diagnostic CT. No pulmonary nodules. No visceral PSMA-avid disease. This is a favorable finding in the context of this patient's disease trajectory.

5. No new sites of PSMA-avid disease identified in bone, lymph nodes, or viscera.

Overall, the pattern of findings suggests a meaningful metabolic response to Lynparza across the osseous and nodal disease burden. Correlation with clinical and biochemical parameters (PSA trend, hemoglobin, performance status) is recommended for comprehensive treatment response assessment.
